items.correct: Designing products usable by the widest possible range of people without special adaptation.
items.random_wrong: Adding multiple language options to packaging.
items.why: Students conflate inclusive with specialist design. Inclusive design widens MAINSTREAM products to work for more people - the OXO Good Grips kitchen tools, designed for arthritic hands, work better for everyone.

items.correct: A drawing showing multiple 2D views (front, side, plan) of an object.
items.misconception: A 3D drawing showing the object at an angle.
items.random_wrong: A drawing in black ink only.
items.why: Students confuse orthographic (multi-view 2D) with isometric (single 3D view). Orthographic projects each face flat onto separate views - used for engineering specification.

items.q: What is the difference between a wood stain and a varnish?
items.correct: Stain colours the wood; varnish provides a protective surface coat.
items.random_wrong: Stain is permanent, varnish always peels off.
items.why: Students confuse colouring (stain penetrates) with protecting (varnish sits on top). Many products combine both, but their primary functions differ. Often stain is applied first, then varnish.

items.q: Whose 1955 Designing for People standardised anthropometric data?
items.why: It's Dreyfuss. “Loewy” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Fitts' Law. “Hick-Hyman Law” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which percentile range do designs typically accommodate?
items.why: The real figure is 95 percentile. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
